title: The Battle of Berlin, April–May 1945
subtitle: The Final Reckoning
author: VeteransLedger Editorial
summary: The Battle of Berlin in April and May 1945 was one of the largest and bloodiest urban battles in military history, ending in the suicide of Adolf Hitler and the unconditional surrender of Nazi Germany.

body.content: By April 1945, the strategic situation for Germany was irretrievable. The Western Allies stood on the Rhine; the Soviet Union's three million men were poised along the Oder-Neisse line, sixty kilometres east of Berlin. Adolf Hitler remained in his underground command bunker beneath the Reich Chancellery, orchestrating phantom operations with divisions that had ceased to exist.

The Soviet plan for the final offensive was drawn up by Marshal Georgy Zhukov (1st Belorussian Front) and Marshal Ivan Konev (1st Ukrainian Front). A third formation, Marshal Konstantin Rokossovsky's 2nd Belorussian Front, would protect the northern flank. Stalin deliberately set Zhukov and Konev against one another, drawing a boundary between their fronts at Lübben — leaving the race to Berlin itself open.

The Vistula-Oder Offensive had established bridgeheads across the Oder river. The final approach to Berlin was blocked by the Seelow Heights — a ridge of bluffs rising 40–50 metres above the Oder floodplain, fortified by General Gotthard Heinrici's Army Group Vistula. Heinrici, the Wehrmacht's foremost expert in defensive tactics, had predicted Zhukov's attack timing and pulled his front-line troops back before the bombardment. When over 9,000 Soviet guns opened fire at 03:00 on 16 April — and Zhukov controversially switched on 143 searchlights to blind the defenders — most fell on empty positions.

The battle for the Heights lasted four days, costing the Soviets approximately 30,000 dead and 750 tanks. German resistance was desperate, and the defences were manned partly by Volkssturm militia and Hitler Youth in addition to regular troops. Heinrici was eventually relieved by Hitler on 28 April for ordering unauthorised withdrawals. The Heights fell on 19 April.

Konev's forces, advancing faster in the south, reached the southern outskirts of Berlin on 20 April — Hitler's birthday — before Zhukov's troops reached the city from the east. By 25 April, Berlin was completely encircled. The city's garrison numbered around 85,000 regular troops supplemented by 40,000 Volkssturm, police, and armed civilians — against 2.5 million Soviet soldiers.

On 24 April, the German 9th Army under General Theodor Busse was encircled south-east of Berlin at Frankfurt an der Oder. Hitler ordered the 9th Army to break through to relieve the city — it could not. He ordered SS-General Felix Steiner to attack southward with 'Army Detachment Steiner' — which existed largely on paper. When Steiner reported he lacked the forces, the scene in the bunker on 22 April was the moment Hitler publicly acknowledged that the war was lost.

The street fighting inside Berlin lasted approximately ten days. The Soviets used Sturmgruppen (assault groups) — combined teams of infantry, engineers, artillery and armour who cleared buildings floor by floor. German defenders took maximum advantage of the rubble and the U-Bahn tunnels. Each city block became a separate battle. Soviet losses to Panzerfausts and SS fanatics defending positions to the last man were severe.

SS units, including foreign volunteers who knew they had nothing to gain from surrender, fought with particular ferocity in the city centre. A battalion of French SS volunteers of the Charlemagne Division was among the last defenders of the Reich Chancellery area.

In the Führerbunker, Hitler married Eva Braun on the night of 28–29 April and dictated his political and personal testaments, blaming the German people and the Jews for the catastrophe. He received news that Heinrich Himmler had been attempting to negotiate surrender with the Western Allies through Count Bernadotte — and had SS General Hermann Fegelein (Himmler's liaison officer and brother-in-law of Eva Braun) shot.

On 30 April 1945, at approximately 15:30, Adolf Hitler shot himself in his private study while Eva Braun took cyanide. Their bodies were burned in the Reich Chancellery garden above the bunker, in accordance with Hitler's instructions. Joseph Goebbels and his wife Magda — who had poisoned their six children — also died in the bunker complex on 1 May.

The garrison of Berlin surrendered on 2 May 1945. General Carl Weidling, the city's last commander, issued the order at 06:00. Germany's unconditional surrender was signed at Reims on 7 May and ratified at Karlshorst, Berlin on 8–9 May 1945 — VE Day.

The Soviet assault on Berlin cost approximately 81,000 Soviet dead and 280,000 wounded. German military casualties in the final battle are estimated at over 100,000 killed, with a further 480,000 captured. An estimated 125,000 Berlin civilians died during the battle and its immediate aftermath. The Red Army's mass rape of German women — estimated by historians at between 95,000 and 130,000 in Berlin alone — represented one of the worst war crimes against civilians in European history.

archival_note: Bundesarchiv-Militärarchiv, Freiburg. RH 2/2616: Army High Command — Berlin defence files, April 1945. Russian State Military Archive (RGVA), Moscow: Frontal operational records, 1st Belorussian Front, April–May 1945. Gedenkstätte Deutscher Widerstand, Berlin.

sources.ref: Bundesarchiv-Militärarchiv, Freiburg. RH 2/2616: OKH Berlin defence records.
Russian State Military Archive (RGVA), Moscow. 1st Belorussian Front operational files, April–May 1945.
Beevor, Antony. Berlin: The Downfall 1945. Viking, 2002.
Le Tissier, Tony. The Battle of Berlin 1945. Jonathan Cape, 1988.
Duffy, Christopher. Red Storm on the Reich: The Soviet March on Germany 1945. Routledge, 1991.
